subject: ap-precalculus
title: Exponential Decay: Medication Modeling, Half-Life & Competing Models
stimulus:
A researcher is studying the elimination of a medication from the bloodstream. A patient receives a single dose and the amount of medication remaining is measured at several times after administration:

| Time $t$ (hours) | Amount $Q$ (mg) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 400 |
| 3 | 263 |
| 7 | 152 |
| 12 | 75 |
| 18 | 29 |

The researcher proposes the exponential decay model $Q(t) = 400 \cdot b^t$ for the amount of medication remaining, in milligrams, at time $t$ hours.

parts.prompt:
(i) Use the data at $t = 0$ and $t = 18$ to find the value of $b$ as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Use the model to determine the half-life of the medication — the time at which the amount remaining equals half the initial dose. Express your answer as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), students must use $400 \cdot b^{18} = 29$ and isolate $b$ by taking an 18th root or using logarithms. Accept $b \approx 0.864$ (accept values in $[0.862, 0.867]$); supporting work must show the root-taking or logarithm step. For part (ii), students solve $400 \cdot b^t = 200$, reducing to $b^t = 0.5$ and applying logarithms to get $t = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(b)} \approx 4.753$ hours (accept values in $[4.7, 4.8]$). First decimal presentation error per question does not earn the point. A student who uses a different data pair from the table receives credit for correct algebra regardless of the resulting $b$ value.

scoring_points.description: Correct value of $b \approx 0.864$ — from $400 \cdot b^{18} = 29$: $b^{18} = \frac{29}{400} = 0.0725$, so $b = (0.0725)^{1/18} \approx 0.864$ (accept values in $[0.862, 0.867]$); supporting work must show the root-taking or logarithm step
wrong_examples: $b = 0.864$ with no supporting work (supporting work required)
common_traps: Forgetting to take the 18th root — $b^{18} = 0.0725$ gives $b$, not $b^{18}$
Decimal presentation error: fewer than 3 decimal places on first occurrence does not earn the point

scoring_points.description: Correct half-life $t \approx 4.753$ hours — solving $400 \cdot b^t = 200 \Rightarrow b^t = 0.5 \Rightarrow t = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(b)} \approx 4.753$ (accept values in $[4.7, 4.8]$ based on $b$ value from part (i)); supporting work must show the logarithm step
wrong_examples: $t = 18/2 = 9$ (erroneously halving the time endpoint rather than solving the equation)
common_traps: Must show the logarithm step — setting up $b^t = 0.5$ alone is insufficient without solving for $t$
Use the $b$ value from part (i) — accept any $t$ consistent with the student's $b$

parts.prompt:
(i) Using the values at $t = 0$ and $t = 18$, find the slope and $y$-intercept of the line through $(0,\ \ln(400))$ and $(18,\ \ln(29))$ on a plot of $\ln(Q)$ versus $t$. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Use the exponential model $Q(t) = 400 \cdot b^t$ with $b$ from part (a) to estimate the amount of medication remaining at $t = 7$ hours. Then compare this estimate to the actual data value $Q = 152$ mg at $t = 7$. Express your estimate as a decimal approximation.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), slope $= \frac{\ln 29 - \ln 400}{18 - 0} \approx \frac{-2.624}{18} \approx -0.146$ (accept $[-0.147, -0.145]$) and $y$-intercept $= \ln(400) \approx 5.991$ (accept $[5.98, 6.00]$); supporting work must show the slope as a ratio of differences of $\ln$ values. For part (ii), $Q(7) = 400 \cdot (0.864)^7 \approx 136$ mg (accept $[130, 145]$ based on $b$ from part (a)). The comparison must note the estimate is less than $152$ mg — the model underestimates at $t = 7$. Award point for correct substitution and a valid comparison stated.

scoring_points.description: Correct slope $\approx -0.146$ and $y$-intercept $\approx 5.991$ — slope $= \frac{\ln(29) - \ln(400)}{18 - 0} = \frac{3.367 - 5.991}{18} \approx -0.146$ (accept $[-0.147, -0.145]$); $y$-intercept $= \ln(400) \approx 5.991$ (accept $[5.98, 6.00]$); supporting work must show slope as a ratio
wrong_examples: slope $= \frac{29 - 400}{18} \approx -20.6$ (used $Q$ values instead of $\ln Q$ values)
common_traps: The plot is $\ln(Q)$ vs $t$, so slope uses $\ln$ values, not raw $Q$ values
Supporting work must show the slope formula with $\ln$ values in the numerator

scoring_points.description: Correct estimate $Q(7) \approx 136$ mg (accept $[130, 145]$ based on $b$ from part (a)) with a stated comparison to the actual value $152$ mg noting the model underestimates

required_elements: comparison to $152$ mg noting underestimate or discrepancy
alternatives.correct_example: $Q(7) = 400 \cdot (0.864)^7 \approx 136$ mg; this underestimates the actual value of $152$ mg at $t = 7$

wrong_examples: Reading $Q(7) = 152$ directly from the table rather than computing from the model
common_traps: Must use the model $Q(t) = 400 \cdot b^t$ not the data table
Comparison must address whether the model overestimates or underestimates — a comparison statement is required

parts.prompt:
(i) A hospital protocol requires a second dose to be administered when the amount of medication in the patient's bloodstream falls below $50$ milligrams. Using the model $Q(t) = 400 \cdot b^t$ with $b$ from part (a), write and solve an inequality to find the earliest time $t$ at which a second dose should be given. Express your answer as a decimal approximation.

(ii) A colleague suggests using a linear model $L(t) = 400 - 20.6t$ to approximate $Q(t)$ for $t \geq 0$. Determine whether $L(t)$ overestimates or underestimates $Q(t)$ for all $t > 0$, and justify your answer using the concavity of $Q$.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), $400 \cdot b^t < 50 \Rightarrow b^t < 1/8 \Rightarrow t > \frac{\ln(1/8)}{\ln(b)} \approx 14.26$ hours (accept $[14.1, 14.5]$ based on $b$ from part (a)). The inequality direction flips when dividing by $\ln(b) < 0$; the rubric checks for the correct direction. For part (ii), $Q$ is concave up (exponential decay function has positive second derivative). $L$ and $Q$ agree at $t = 0$ only; since $Q$ is concave up, its graph lies above any tangent line and above any secant line from $t = 0$, so $L$ overestimates $Q$ for $t > 0$ in the context of the problem (where $L > Q$). Award the point for correct conclusion plus reasoning referencing the concave-up shape.

scoring_points.description: Correct inequality $t > \frac{\ln(1/8)}{\ln(b)} \approx 14.26$ hours (accept $[14.1, 14.5]$) — from $400 \cdot b^t < 50 \Rightarrow b^t < 1/8$, then applying $\ln$ and noting $\ln(b) < 0$ flips the inequality; supporting work must show the inequality setup and the logarithm step

required_elements: logarithm step with inequality direction correctly reversed

wrong_examples: $t < 14.26$ (inequality direction not reversed when dividing by negative $\ln(b)$)
$400 \cdot b^t = 50$ solved as an equation without inequality setup
common_traps: Dividing by $\ln(b) < 0$ reverses the inequality direction — $t > \frac{\ln(1/8)}{\ln(b)}$, not $t <$
Must show the inequality setup with $< 50$, not just solve the equation

scoring_points.description: Correct conclusion '$L$ overestimates $Q$ for $t > 0$' with justification referencing the concave-up shape of $Q$ — since $Q$ is an exponential decay function, $Q'' > 0$ (concave up); $L$ and $Q$ agree at $t = 0$, and because $Q$ is concave up the graph of $Q$ lies below any line tangent or secant from $t = 0$, so $L(t) > Q(t)$ for $t > 0$

required_elements: justification using concave up nature of $Q$
alternatives.correct_example: $Q$ is concave up (exponential functions have this property), so a linear function starting at $Q(0) = L(0) = 400$ lies above $Q$ for $t > 0$; therefore $L$ overestimates $Q$.

wrong_examples: '$L$ underestimates $Q$' (incorrect — $Q$ falls faster than $L$ due to concavity)
Stating '$L$ overestimates' without any reference to concavity or shape of $Q$
common_traps: Must reference concavity of $Q$ (concave up) to earn the point — a purely numerical comparison without reasoning about concavity is insufficient
Concave up means the curve falls away from a straight line tangent — linear model stays above the curve for $t > 0$
